data.title: The focus of AI payment is not the payment itself.
data.description: "Reorder the milk tea I bought from that shop yesterday." If one day you could really say this to your phone, and it would find the merchant, confirm the product, place the order, pay, and arrange delivery, you probably wouldn't first think of a payment revolution. You'd mostly just think that your phone is finally starting to act as a capable assistant. This might be a more appropriate starting point for our discussion of AI payments today. Most people's understanding of AI payments isn't about giving AI a wallet, nor is it about letting a robot decide how to spend money. What people truly experience is whether a single sentence can directly become a transaction. You state your needs, and the system handles the rest. Therefore, what's truly worth discussing about AI payment isn't the word "payment" itself, as that term is too abstract and distant. For most internet users, the immediate experience might be: an app or AI assistant can handle your shopping needs directly when you express them in plain language. If we only look at the Chinese market, AI payment isn't actually that unfamiliar. The reason is simple. Chinese users already live within a very mature mobile internet ecosystem: food delivery, e-commerce, maps, ride-hailing, local services, mobile payment, and instant delivery—these things are inherently interconnected. Often, what users want isn't a completely new payment method, but rather to avoid having to switch between apps and repeatedly click the same buttons. In China, AI payment is more like an upgrade to the e-commerce shopping experience than a complete rebuild of the payment system from scratch. Alibaba's upgrade of the Qwen App in January this year exemplifies this direction. According to the official introduction, Qwen has integrated with services within the Alibaba ecosystem, including Taobao, Taobao Flash Sale, Alipay, Fliggy, and Gaode Maps. Users can order food, complete payments in chat, and plan and book trips with just a sentence. It seems like AI has become smarter, but more accurately, it's the existing capabilities of the platform that have been reorganized. This is why AI payment is most easily implemented in China not through grand visions, but through everyday scenarios. For example, "Please buy another box of the printer paper I bought last week and deliver it to my office." Or, "Order another delivery from that restaurant yesterday and have it delivered to my home." Or, "Book a restaurant close to the office, for four people, with a budget of under 500 yuan." Users understand these prompts immediately. Because the AI ​​here isn't some mysterious new financial tool; it's simply a smarter shopping assistant. Whatever the people need, the endlessly competitive Chinese internet giants will do it. Today, there are roughly three implementation paths surrounding AI payments. One is to let AI watch the screen and operate on your behalf, clicking buttons, filling in information, and submitting processes like a human. The advantage of this approach is that it can be tested anywhere, but the disadvantages are also obvious: problems easily arise when the page changes, and key parts often require your final confirmation. For example, the Doubao phone, which was immediately targeted by WeChat upon release. Another approach is for the platform to simply hand over its service capabilities directly to AI. For large Chinese companies, this path is actually the smoothest. Because payment, merchants, maps, orders, and delivery are already under their control. AI doesn't need to mimic user clicks; it can directly access the capabilities within the system. In terms of user experience, it's "I said something, and it actually did it for me." Examples include Alibaba's Qianwen and ByteDance's Doubao. The third approach is even more difficult, namely the cross-platform, cross-website, and cross-company approach. At this stage, the issue is not just whether the product is easy to use, but whether others are willing to let your AI represent the user to perform this task. Currently, no Chinese internet company seems to be attempting to challenge this "hard" model. Today, China's greatest advantage clearly lies in the first two approaches, especially the second. This is why, for Chinese users, AI payment may not seem groundbreaking; their overall feeling is likely not "AI payment has arrived," but rather "mobile shopping is more convenient." The situation is different overseas. However, once the perspective shifts overseas, things are somewhat different. While overseas markets also have e-commerce, food delivery, maps, and payments, they lack the highly integrated platform structure found in China. Service entry points, merchant systems, payment tools, and fulfillment networks are often controlled by different companies. Therefore, when AI wants to complete a transaction on behalf of a user, it faces not just the question of "can the order be placed?", but also "will others accept you as an AI agent?" This is why overseas AI payments have suddenly become so complex. A typical example is the conflict between Amazon and Perplexity. Perplexity's Comet browser touted AI shopping as a key selling point, stating in its official description that it can help users compare products, read reviews, and go all the way to checkout. This aligns with many people's most intuitive imagination of AI shopping: instead of browsing website after website, AI will pick and buy for you. But the problem lies precisely here. According to court documents dated March 9, 2026, a core argument Amazon made in the lawsuit was that Perplexity's AI agent accessed user accounts and attempted to perform actions "with the user's consent, but without Amazon's consent." Amazon's reasons for disagreeing are largely the same as WeChat's reasons for disagreeing with Doubao Mobile: authorizing AI doesn't mean the website also authorizes it. Your willingness to let it buy things for you doesn't mean the platform is willing to allow a third-party agent into its system to initiate transactions on your behalf. While the official explanation is based on considerations of customer privacy and security, the core concern is that AI shopping directly diverts billions of dollars of advertising revenue from its sellers. After all, AI shopping is a straightforward, male-centric approach; it goes straight for the product and doesn't care about ads. In China, these kinds of conflicts are often resolved by the platform's integrated capabilities; however, overseas, once AI needs to complete transactions across websites, merchants, and payment networks, these issues become unavoidable. Therefore, overseas, the primary question for AI payments isn't "how easy it is to use," but rather "whether this can be completed in a closed-loop end-to-end manner." This is why Google, Stripe, and Coinbase have recently been focusing their efforts on this area. They all see the same problem: in the past, internet transactions assumed that the order was placed by a person, who was sitting in front of the screen, browsing, confirming, and paying on their own. But once the person placing the order becomes an authorized AI, the original assumption no longer holds. How does the website know that this AI is really sent by you? How can the merchant confirm that it is not making random purchases? How should the payment institution judge whether the money should be released? Google wants to solve the trust problem first. In September 2025, it launched AP2, not to teach AI how to make payments, but to answer the question: when AI initiates a payment on behalf of a user, what does the system rely on to believe that it is genuine? Google's solution is to add a verifiable "authorization certificate" to such transactions. It designed two key elements in AP2: Cart Mandate, which can be understood as the user's signature confirmation of the specific purchase; and Payment Mandate, which is for the payment network and card issuer, informing them that this is a transaction initiated by an agent, whether the user was present at the time, and the context of the transaction. Google later integrated this system with PayPal to create a more complete merchant solution: merchants can use their own conversational shopping assistant to serve users, and at the payment step, PayPal Agent takes over, using AP2 to complete the authorization and payment process. Ultimately, Google's approach addresses the fundamental question of "why trust AI to place orders." Stripe's approach is closer to merchants and platforms. It actually involves two layers. The first layer is ACP, or Agentic Commerce Protocol, launched in September 2025 with OpenAI. You can think of it as a set of "AI-readable checkout standards." Stripe doesn't want AI to mimic humans clicking web pages and filling out forms; instead, it wants merchants to proactively open up their capabilities regarding products, inventory, and checkout, allowing AI to initiate purchases using a standardized interface. The advantage of this is that merchants remain primarily responsible for orders and fulfillment—product display, order processing, and risk control are all still in their hands—but they don't need to rebuild their systems for each AI platform. Stripe later launched the Agentic Commerce Suite, making catalog access, checkout, and payment a one-stop tool to lower the barrier to entry for merchants. However, Stripe also saw a completely different scenario. It's not "AI helping people buy things," but "software paying for software directly." Therefore, in March 2026, it launched Machine Payments. In this scenario, AI doesn't buy a Coke, but rather APIs, data, computing power, content access rights, or even call services on a per-use basis. Stripe's solution is that merchants can directly convert their APIs to pay-per-use rates, as low as 0.01 USDC, with the money going into a Stripe account and ultimately settled and reconciled using Stripe's familiar methods. For agents, there's no need to register an account, apply for an API key, or go through a bunch of manual processes; they can make payments while making calls. In other words, Stripe is building both an AI shopping checkout system and a micro-payment track between machines. Coinbase takes a different approach. It's more like assuming that in the future, many AIs won't be "shopping assistants" but rather "software with wallets." Therefore, in February 2026, it launched Agentic Wallets. The core of this isn't to make AI better at browsing websites, but to give AI a real wallet that can spend, receive, and transact, and this wallet also has security boundaries. Coinbase's system comprises three parts: the wallet infrastructure itself, the x402 machine payment protocol, and pre-packaged capabilities such as deposits, transfers, transactions, and earnings management. Its primary focus is addressing another type of problem: if an AI wants to purchase computing power, data, pay API fees, or complete automated transactions on-chain, can it avoid requiring manual confirmation each time? Coinbase's official documentation refers to this agent as an "independent economic agent." This terminology is somewhat strong, but the meaning is clear: Coinbase doesn't aim to make AI more like a shopping assistant, but rather to enable AI itself to become a software entity capable of receiving and spending, settling accounts, and transacting. If we consider these three approaches together, the differences become clear. Google aims to first solve the problem of "how do you prove that this transaction was truly authorized by me?" Stripe is more concerned with "how merchants can connect their products and checkout capabilities to AI." Coinbase, on the other hand, has gone a step further, addressing "how software can handle its own transactions if the payer is software itself." This is why, while they all appear to be working on AI payments, their focuses differ. Google is more like adding a layer of trust to cross-platform intermediary transactions; Stripe aims to integrate AI shopping and AI-driven service calls into existing commercial networks; Coinbase, however, is betting on a world where software directly pays for software. Two stories. At this point, the difference is clear. In China, AI payments are more like a natural extension of a mature internet system. The platform has already organized merchants, payments, delivery, maps, and local services. AI's role is to rearrange these capabilities, allowing users to go from "operating themselves" to "simply speaking." Overseas, AI payments are more like a belated catch-up. Because no platform naturally controls the entire chain, once AI is to actually complete transactions for users, it immediately encounters real-world problems such as whether websites will approve it, whether merchants will accept it, whether payment institutions will recognize it, and who will be responsible if problems arise. In China, it's more like pushing the online shopping experience a step further. Overseas, it's more like renegotiating the rules for participation by multiple parties in internet software and online consumption scenarios. This is precisely why Chinese users' first reaction to AI payment might be, "This function is quite convenient"; while overseas markets' perception of AI payment is more like, "The original way of online transactions may need to be changed." Returning to the initial scenario: "Order the milk tea I bought from that shop yesterday and have it delivered." The truly interesting part of this sentence isn't the word "payment," but rather that it delegates many tasks that you would normally have to do manually to the system. It needs to know which shop, which item, what specifications, where to deliver it, and what payment method to use. Payment is certainly important, but it's just the most insignificant step in the whole process. Therefore, the focus of AI payments is not the payment itself. What people are truly concerned about isn't which protocol is used in the backend, whether it's on-chain settlement, whether it uses stablecoins, or whether it uses encryption algorithms. Ultimately, people will only use a very simple standard to judge its usefulness: "I told the AI ​​something, can it actually get the job done for me?"

data.title: CLARITY or negative news regarding yield isolation: DeFi TRUMP team suspected of dumping tokens.
data.description:
Headline

▌Analyst: The CLARITY Act May Put Negative Pressure on Yield-Separated DeFi Tokens

On March 30th, the latest version of the crypto bill, the CLARITY Act, has attracted much attention mainly due to its regulatory provisions targeting stablecoins. A report released by 10x Research states that, in practical implementation, the bill may have the most severe impact on DeFi and its related tokens. The core content of the bill's proposal is to prohibit providing investment returns and rewards, as well as any similar forms of returns, to stablecoin balances. This effectively ends the attribute of stablecoins as on-chain savings products, redefining them as pure payment and circulation tools. 10x Research founder Marcus Thilan pointed out: "This means that investment return rights will clearly return to centralization." The reason is that the bill will bring yield-based businesses under the control of banks, money market funds, and various compliant licensed institutions, significantly compressing the competitive space for native crypto platforms in terms of yield returns.

While some initially believed DeFi would benefit, this shift in the industry landscape will also impact DeFi. Tiren explained that the previous market logic assumed that if centralized platforms stopped offering investment returns, user funds would flow to the on-chain DeFi ecosystem. However, this assumption rested on the premise that DeFi was not subject to similar regulatory constraints. In reality, the regulatory framework of the CLARITY Act is likely to extend to front-end interfaces and various token models; especially when projects generate transaction fees and governance rights resemble traditional equity, regulators will intervene comprehensively. On March 29th, according to on-chain analyst Ai Yi, the Trump team is suspected of selling over $16.06 million worth of Trump. Bitgo's escrow address deposited 5.48 million TRUMP tokens into OKX in the past two hours. Tracing the source of the funds, it was found that the TRUMP Team Allocation address (i.e., team shares) deposited 18.14 million tokens two months ago, which was worth $81.64 million at the time. As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $65,869.48, a 24-hour change of -0.8%; ETH price is $1,982.69, a 24-hour change of -0.7%; BNB price is $605.31, a 24-hour change of -1.0%. SOL price is $81.28, a 24-hour change of -1.0%; DOGE price is $0.09027, a 24-hour change of -0.6%; XRP price is $1.33, a 24-hour change of -0.5%; TRX price is $0.322, a 24-hour change of +1.8%. WLFI price is $0.09754, 24-hour change +0.3%; HYPE price is $37.92, 24-hour change -3.9%. Policy: Turkey removes crypto tax clause, previously proposed 0.3% transaction tax. On March 29, the Turkish parliament removed the crypto asset-related tax clause during the review of a comprehensive bill. This clause previously proposed levying a 0.3% transaction tax on crypto transactions conducted through service providers and withholding tax on crypto earnings. This adjustment stemmed from strong opposition from the opposition. Officials stated that the relevant content may be resubmitted in the form of separate legislation in the future.

▌Iranian Foreign Ministry Spokesperson: US Proposals Through Media Outlets Are Extremely Unreasonable

On March 29 local time, Iranian Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Baghae stated that the relevant proposals submitted to Iran by the United States through media outlets were extremely unreasonable. Baghae stated in a media interview that day that these proposals involved Iran's fundamental rights and did not reflect a goodwill or serious diplomatic attitude. He also stated that Iran must rely on its own capabilities to ensure its security and will use all means to prevent further attacks. Baghae stated that Iran's military actions will not target Arab countries, but only US military bases and assets used in the operation against Iran, and stated that the current conflict is not a war between the American people and Iran, but a conflict imposed on the region.

... (CCTV) Blockchain Applications Gnosis and Zisk Announce the Launch of the "Ethereum Economic Zone" Rollup Framework, Jointly Funded by the Ethereum Foundation On March 29th, a group of Ethereum projects announced a new initiative aimed at addressing a growing problem with Ethereum: its ecosystem is becoming overly fragmented. At the EthCC conference in Cannes, France, this project, called the "Ethereum Economic Zone" (EEZ), was officially unveiled, aiming to achieve more seamless collaboration between Ethereum's numerous scaling networks (i.e., Layer 2, or L2). The framework was jointly developed by Gnosis, Zisk, and the Ethereum Foundation. Gnosis is a development team with long-standing experience in Ethereum infrastructure, while Zisk focuses on zero-knowledge proof technology. For years, Ethereum has relied on L2 networks for scaling, but these networks have often operated independently like "islands." Users typically need to transfer assets between different networks via cross-chain bridges, a process that is often slow, costly, and risky. Meanwhile, developers often need to repeatedly build the same tools on each network. EEZ aims to change this, allowing these networks to operate as a unified system. Simply put, it will allow applications and transactions on different Ethereum networks to interact instantly—without cross-chain bridges—while still relying on Ethereum's core security. This release comes amid renewed debate about Ethereum's long-standing reliance on an L2 scaling path. Ethereum co-founder Vitalik Buterin recently stated that with persistent fragmentation and user experience issues, the ecosystem may need to re-evaluate its L2-centric roadmap. EEZ appears to be a direct response to these issues, attempting to integrate the entire network ecosystem by unifying liquidity, infrastructure, and user processes, rather than continuing to add fragmented chains. Cryptocurrency ▌Walmart Holding Company OnePay Adds More Than Ten Crypto Tokens to Its Services

On March 30, Walmart-owned OnePay added more than ten crypto tokens to its services. An executive in charge of digital assets stated that these tokens "meet the high standards set by bank application customers." Since launching its initial crypto platform, which included Bitcoin and Ethereum, in January, OnePay added SUI, Polygon, and Arbitrum this Thursday, just days after listing 10 tokens including Solana, Cardano, Bitcoin Cash, and PAXGold. The company also offers a digital wallet that users can use at Walmart stores and online. According to its latest annual report, Walmart's U.S. operations are projected to generate $462.4 billion in net sales in fiscal year 2025.

... BNP Paribas expands its crypto asset product line, launching six crypto asset ETNs including Bitcoin and Ethereum. On March 29th, BNP Paribas officially announced the launch of six crypto asset-related ETNs (Exchange Traded Notes), providing investors with a channel to indirectly allocate assets such as Bitcoin and Ethereum without directly holding these crypto assets. These products are based on the performance of crypto assets, issued by several well-known asset management institutions, and comply with the MiFID II regulatory framework to enhance investor protection. It is understood that these ETNs will be open for subscription to individual clients, business clients, and private banking clients in France starting March 30, 2026, with plans to gradually expand to a wider range of wealth management clients.

▌Polygon Founder: Polygon On-Chain Stablecoin Transaction Volume Reaches Nearly 160 Million Last Week, Setting a New Record

On March 29th, Polygon founder Sandeep Nailwal posted on the X platform that Polygon's stablecoin transaction volume reached 159.9 million last week, setting a new record. Simultaneously, key indicators such as Polygon's on-chain stablecoin transaction volume and the number of active addresses all increased, indicating that a "flywheel effect" of network growth is forming. Furthermore, Polygon's PoS network checkpoints have reached 100,000, and network usage continues to expand. Sandeep Nailwal added that against the backdrop of a resurgence of the "crypto is dead" narrative, teams that continuously build often develop valuable products during the cycle, and stated that Polygon is accelerating the construction of the future of on-chain payments.

... Michael Saylor, founder of Bitcoin treasury firm Strategy, stated on the X platform that the volatility of perpetual preferred stock STRC has been lower than that of all companies in the S&P 500 index and major asset classes over the past 30 days, while achieving a dividend yield of approximately 11.5%. In the current market environment, STRC exhibits characteristics of both low volatility and high returns, potentially offering investors an allocation option different from traditional assets. However, Michael Saylor has not yet released any information regarding Bitcoin Trackers. Historically, Strategy typically discloses its Bitcoin holdings the day after such news is released.

▌Lido DAO Proposes $20 Million One-Time LDO Buyback

On March 30th, the Lido DAO ecosystem operations team proposed using up to 10,000 stETH (approximately $20 million) from the DAO treasury to buy back LDO tokens, stating that the current valuation is at a historical low. At current prices, this buyback could absorb approximately 8% of the circulating supply of LDO. This proposal is independent of Lido's yet-to-be-launched NEST automatic buyback program, which has not yet been triggered under current market conditions. LDO fell to an all-time low of $0.27 on March 7th and is currently trading at approximately $0.31.

...

▌Data: Total Market Value of Tokenized Assets on HyperEVM Exceeds $1.3 Billion, USDC Ranks First

On March 30, according to Token Terminal, the total market value of tokenized assets on HyperEVM exceeded $1.3 billion, with USDC issued by Circle firmly holding the top spot. Tokenized assets include: stablecoins, funds, stocks, and commodities.

Important Economic Developments

▌Markets Begin to Reprice Interest Rate Hike Expectations, Inflation Concerns and Geopolitics Reshape the Fed's Path

On March 29, "Describing the recent changes in market expectations for central bank monetary policy as a '180-degree turn' is insufficient." Just a few weeks ago, the market was still expecting the Fed to cut interest rates multiple times in 2026, but now it is clearly beginning to price in a possible rate hike this year.

The latest data from the CME FedWatch Tool shows that the probability of the federal funds rate remaining above the current 3.50%-3.75% range by the end of this year is close to 30%, while the probability of a rate cut has fallen to 2.9%. This shift in expectations is primarily driven by inflation concerns stemming from the energy market. Since the escalation of tensions in the Middle East at the end of February, Brent crude oil prices have risen from around $70 per barrel to around $111 currently. Meanwhile, long-term yields on U.S. Treasury bonds have also risen sharply, with the 10-year Treasury yield rising from less than 4% a few weeks ago to approximately 4.40%. The Crypto is Macro Now newsletter states, "Food and energy prices will unfortunately continue to rise and remain high for some time, at least until the Middle East shipping chaos is resolved. Even if a peace agreement is reached tomorrow (which is unlikely), it will take at least several months to ease the situation." ▌Sources: Senate Hearings for Fed Chair Nominee Warsh to be Held as Early as the Week of April 13th On March 30th, Punchbowl reported that two sources familiar with the plans revealed that the Senate Banking Committee plans to hold hearings for Kevin Warsh's nomination as Federal Reserve Chair as early as the week of April 13th. The specific date is uncertain and will depend on whether Warsh submits complete materials to the committee. (Jinshi) On March 30th, according to CME's "FedWatch": the probability of the Fed raising interest rates by 25 basis points in April is 2.1%, and the probability of keeping rates unchanged is 97.9%. The probability of the Fed raising rates by a cumulative 25 basis points by June is 8.8%, the probability of raising rates by a cumulative 50 basis points is 0.1%, and the probability of keeping rates unchanged is 91.1%. (Jinshi Encyclopedia) Can Bitcoin still be called "digital gold" during geopolitical crises? Every time a geopolitical crisis occurs, gold prices rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been confirmed by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin exhibits an asymmetry: it falls with stocks but doesn't rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an overabundance of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation). Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," capable of functioning when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold but a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread adoption of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

Foreword

If different wallets are combined into one entity, Satoshi Nakamoto, the anonymous creator of Bitcoin, is the largest holder, holding 1.096 million Bitcoins (approximately $77 billion). As for a single address, the wallet holding the most Bitcoins is a cold wallet on the Binance exchange, holding nearly 250,000 Bitcoins.

Bitcoin, which began as a niche project for cryptography enthusiasts, has evolved into a globally significant financial asset, held by numerous companies, individuals, and government agencies. Bitcoin's immense appeal stems from its decentralized nature. This ensures that the Bitcoin network is not completely controlled by any single individual; even the most determined attacker would find it virtually impossible, as the cost would be extremely high, to gain control of the entire network. The maximum supply of 21 million Bitcoins is a fundamental element of the project's design, ensuring its scarcity and preventing its value from being inflated through increased issuance. Considering that approximately 3.7 million Bitcoins are permanently held in inaccessible wallets, the actual total supply of Bitcoin is even lower. That's all for today. See you in the next article.

data.title: US-Iran war: Why is Türkiye dumping gold?
data.description:
A recent figure that has astonished global financial markets has surfaced: The Central Bank of Turkey reduced its gold reserves by approximately 58.4 tons in just two weeks, worth over $8 billion. Specifically, it reduced by 6 tons in the week of March 13th and plummeted by 52.4 tons in the week of March 20th. The weekly data from the Central Bank of Turkey clearly illustrates this scenario: from March 13th to 19th, the market value of its gold reserves plummeted from $134.1 billion to $116.2 billion, a weekly loss of nearly $18 billion; while during the same period, its foreign exchange reserves (excluding gold) actually rebounded by $5.8 billion. This stark contrast clearly reveals the manipulation of "exchanging gold for foreign exchange." Over the past decade, Turkey has been one of the world's most aggressive gold buyers, accumulating gold reserves from 116 tons in 2011 to over 820 tons. Why then, within two weeks, did they suddenly sell off such a large amount of their hard-earned wealth? The answer is simple: survival.

The Spark: A War That Forced Turkey into a "Perfect Storm"

On February 28, the United States and Israel jointly launched a military operation codenamed "Epic Fury," bombing Iranian nuclear facilities, military bases, and government buildings.

Iran retaliated immediately, effectively blocking the Strait of Hormuz—through which 20% of the world's seaborne oil and 20% of LNG trade pass. Brent crude oil prices surged from $73 per barrel before the war to over $106, an increase of more than 40%, which the International Energy Agency defined as "the most severe global energy security challenge in history." For most countries, this was merely a shock; but for Turkey, it was an existential crisis. Turkey imports 90% of its oil and 98% of its natural gas. A $10 increase in oil prices per barrel increases the current account deficit by $4.5 billion to $7 billion. Based on post-war oil prices, the annual energy import bill could surge by approximately $15 billion. The more devastating blow came on March 24th—Israel bombed Iran's South Pars gas field, prompting Iran to halt natural gas exports to Turkey. Iran is Turkey's second-largest pipeline gas supplier, accounting for approximately 13% to 14% of its gas imports. The pipeline's 25-year contract was set to expire in July 2026, and the war directly dashed any hopes of renewal. In short, Turkey's predicament is: its energy bills have suddenly doubled, its key gas supply has been directly cut off, and it has no equivalent alternative in the short term.

The transmission chain: Foreign exchange reserves couldn't hold on first

Energy imports need to be settled in US dollars, importers are scrambling for dollars, and the lira is plummeting in response.

In the 16 trading days since the outbreak of the conflict, the lira has hit a record low against the US dollar 11 times in a row, reaching about 44.35 lira to 1 US dollar on March 25. Behind this is the accelerated withdrawal of foreign investors: within three weeks, foreign capital flowed out of Turkish bonds by as much as $4.7 billion, the stock market by $1.2 billion, and arbitrage trading positions shrank from a record $61.2 billion in January to below $45 billion. The Turkish central bank was thus forced to launch a "lira defense war." In the first week of March alone, it sold more than $8 billion in foreign exchange. In the three weeks ending March 19, the central bank had cumulatively depleted approximately $25-30 billion of its foreign exchange reserves. Net reserves, after deducting swaps, plummeted from $54.3 billion before the war to $43 billion. Turkey's weekly data fully documents this process: foreign exchange reserves (excluding gold) fell from $55 billion on March 6 to $47.8 billion on March 13—foreign exchange reserves were used first. By March 19, foreign exchange reserves had rebounded to $53.6 billion, but gold reserves simultaneously plummeted from $134.1 billion to $116.2 billion—foreign exchange reserves were running out, and gold was being used. This is a textbook example of an emergency defense sequence of "using foreign exchange first, then gold." Image: Foreign Exchange Data Released by the Central Bank of Turkey The essence of a gold swap is "exchanging gold for currency, with redemption upon maturity." The central bank delivers gold to a counterparty (usually a primary investment bank) in exchange for an equivalent amount of US dollars, while simultaneously signing a forward contract agreeing to buy back the gold at a slightly higher price in the future. It is a short-term financing activity, not a permanent liquidation. Central banks choose swaps rather than outright sales for at least three reasons. First, to maintain a long-term position. If the surge in oil prices is judged to be only a temporary shock, a swap can provide immediate relief, allowing for redemption of the gold later, preventing the loss of a decade's worth of accumulated wealth. Second, it reduces the impact on gold prices. A direct dump of 60 tons of gold would trigger a precipitous drop in the market, significantly reducing the value of Turkey's remaining gold reserves of over $100 billion. Swaps, conducted quietly in the over-the-counter market, have a much smaller impact. Third, it provides a buffer at the domestic political level. Gold is seen as an "anti-inflation symbol" by the Turkish people; announcing a large-scale gold sale could easily trigger panic, while swaps can technically maintain a degree of ambiguity. The reason this operation was completed so quickly within two weeks is due to a key pre-emptive arrangement: Turkey holds approximately 111 tons of gold, worth about $30 billion, in the Bank of England. This gold can be used for foreign exchange intervention without logistical constraints—it can be directly pledged and converted into cash in the City of London without the need for cross-border physical transport. [Image of gold under pressure on gold prices] Turkey has a historical pattern: selling gold during crises and buying it back afterward. In the 2018 lira crisis, the 2020 pandemic, and the 2023 earthquake—each time, the central bank reduced its gold holdings to provide liquidity, but subsequently resumed accumulation. Analysts generally believe that the March 2026 operation continued this pattern. However, this judgment rests on a core premise: the war cannot be prolonged. Swap agreements come with holding costs and interest. If the war continues, energy prices remain anchored above $100 for an extended period, and Turkey's foreign exchange earnings cannot cover soaring energy bills, then these "temporary swaps" will never be redeemed, effectively becoming a "permanent fire sale." Therefore, in the coming weeks, if the fighting continues, Turkey will need to continue using its $135 billion gold reserves as a lifeline. While Turkey tends to use gold as collateral to obtain foreign exchange liquidity, these transactions still substantially increase downward pressure on the gold market. In the London over-the-counter market, when the Turkish central bank transfers tens of tons of gold as collateral to international counterparties (such as investment banks) in exchange for US dollars, these financial institutions receiving the gold typically engage in corresponding short selling or selling operations in the spot or futures derivatives markets to hedge their own position risks. Therefore, the liquidity of this gold ultimately transmits to the market, indirectly increasing supply and depressing prices.

Conclusion

The Turkish central bank's divestment of 60 tons of gold in two weeks was not panic or speculation, but rather a rational self-rescue by a country highly dependent on energy imports after its allies bombed its largest energy supplier, facing a triple blow of foreign exchange depletion, lira depreciation, and natural gas supply disruption.

The Turkish central bank's divestment of 60 tons of gold in two weeks was not panic or speculation, but a rational self-rescue by a country that heavily relies on energy imports after its allies bombed its largest energy supplier.

data.title: Quantum computing is on its way to changing science: An in-depth look at IBM's latest breakthrough
data.description: As the scientific community eagerly anticipates larger and more powerful quantum computers, IBM and a group of researchers attempted to prove that these systems are already operational today. They succeeded. A preprint paper uploaded to the arXiv platform on Wednesday shows that scientists from IBM, two national laboratories, and three universities have successfully used a quantum computer to simulate a process that is imperceptible to the naked eye but has applications in materials science. Researchers used neutron scattering (a technique that allows a neutron beam to penetrate a sample) to measure the properties of a magnetic crystal and directly compared the results with simulations run on an IBM quantum computer. Ultimately, the quantum processor successfully demonstrated the expected behavior of the crystal. If this description seems a bit complex, consider the researchers' own interpretation: Los Alamos National Laboratory physicist Alan Shay stated that this achievement "raises the bar for expectations regarding the capabilities of quantum computers." (Left: Results of a neutron scattering experiment; Right: Results of a quantum computer simulation from IBM.) Image source: [Image URL: https://img.jinse.com.cn/7443907_image3.png] Quantum-level materials systems are extremely complex, often proving difficult for traditional computers to model. Quantum computers have successfully accomplished this task, signifying that such systems are becoming powerful enough to aid in the development of new materials. This also indirectly confirms the promising application prospects of quantum technology in materials science—a discipline that underpins almost all modern inventions, from medical devices and semiconductors to batteries. The application scenarios for quantum computing are gradually becoming clearer. Earlier this month, IBM released a data center blueprint outlining plans to integrate quantum computers with existing GPUs and CPUs. Beyond materials science, this technology will also have a profound impact on the financial and pharmaceutical industries. Some industry optimists believe it can significantly reduce energy consumption for high-performance computing tasks. Currently, industry experts and investors in the quantum field have lowered their expectations. Before quantum computers achieve widespread commercialization, they cannot be considered truly "usable." To do so, large-scale scaling is essential. Despite this, the capability demonstrated by IBM in its latest experiment was originally expected to be realized only with the advent of large-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computers—machines that can continue to function even if individual components fail or are interfered with. Just as traditional computers encode basic information using bits, quantum computers rely on qubits. However, there is a key difference: qubits are typically generated by manipulating and measuring particles such as photons, electrons, or trapped ions. Unlike traditional bits, qubits are extremely sensitive to environmental changes—anything from heat to electromagnetic interference can disrupt their fragile quantum states, causing computer malfunctions. IBM aims to deliver its first fault-tolerant quantum supercomputer, codenamed "Starling," in 2029, with a projected processing power 20,000 times that of current quantum computers. The next three years may—and inevitably will—see many changes. IBM's latest experiment is just the beginning.

data.title: AI Agents and On-Chain Credit: A Conversation with Legendary Investor Santiago Santos
data.description:
Author: The Rollup; Translator: Plain Language Blockchain

In the wilderness of cryptocurrency, some are busy chasing the myth of 100x returns, while others are quietly changing the rules. When a former angel investor takes off his suit and bluntly states that "most tokens are no longer investable," is this just obsessive arrogance, or a deep analysis of the industry's truth?

This article provides an in-depth look at the latest interview with legendary investor Santiago. He not only reveals why AI is "robbing" the genius minds of cryptocurrencies, but also discloses for the first time his ambitious "contrarian investment" plan: instead of developing projects from scratch, he will acquire traditional telecommunications and financial groups to transform tens of millions of real users with stablecoins and DeFi. From the explosive growth of commodity tokenization to on-the-spot research in Latin America, this is a storm of thought about "dimensional reduction attacks" and "value return."

If you want to see through the true bottom line of institutional investors entering the market, this 4000-word article is definitely not to be missed.

I. Role Growth: From Angel Investor to Crypto "Chameleon"

【Host】: Today we have invited an old friend of the program, a legendary figure in the crypto investment world. To be honest, we always feel very honored to have him by our side.

【Host】: Yes, this is Santiago. Santiago, long time no see, how have you been?

【Santiago】: I'm not dead yet. Aren't you tired of my old face yet?

【Host】: Of course not. However, our marketing team has prepared a very surprising headline for you today—"Isn't Santiago the biggest crypto bear on Earth?" We've heard that you haven't put out a single angel investment check this year, and even that the performance of your tokens will be worse than stocks. Look at you now, you don't even have your signature outfit. What happened?

【Santiago】: I prefer to be an invisible dragon. In the financial markets, you have to constantly build yourself up so that others can't easily label you. I've certainly lived long enough and seen several cycles, but I'm not a "super bear." I'm still optimistic about crypto technology, but I think the current near-current tokens are also not in an "investment" state.

But this doesn't mean the industry is consuming it. On the contrary, adoption is growing like crazy. When I arrived at the venue yesterday morning, the queues reminded me of TSA security at the airport. More importantly, the institutional growth is real. Look at the people in this room, how many of you do you think are attending a cryptocurrency conference for the first time?

**Host:** Indeed, life is full of surprises. **Santi:** This is a signal of the times. Many people come here no longer to inquire about which worthless coin to buy or to chase 100x returns. They are here because their CEOs have given them a death order: "Guys, you have to figure this out, or we'll be left behind." We just finished listening to the speech by the CEO of Western Union. You know my past impression of them—it's a long-established company with strong value protection. But I asked him, "How serious is Western Union about stablecoins?" If you listen carefully to his tone on stage, you'll find that this is completely different from the previous "symbolic disagreements." I predict they will demonstrate the future power of stablecoins in their annual profit and loss statement. By paying with stablecoins, they could potentially save billions of dollars annually on float. This is insane, considering that just a few quarters ago, the CEO publicly stated on a conference call that he didn't believe in stablecoins. [Host]: What made him change his mind so quickly? [Santi]: Competitive pressure and institutional FOMO (panic buying). He saw Visa jump in, saw Mastercard complete large related acquisitions, and saw BlackRock enter the market. One of my analyses at the time was: observing publicly traded companies that mentioned cryptocurrency in earnings calls and actually implemented strategies, mostly companies founded by relevant founders, these stocks significantly outperformed the S&P 500. That's the power of reality.

II. 24/7 Trading Narrative: Hyperliquid and the Tokenization Future of Commodities

【Host】: Speaking of high-performing assets, the S&P 500 has been performing reliably recently, but a very interesting consolidation case has emerged in the crypto market—Hyperliquid. This seems to be something you've been thinking about—projects not in the "bear market basket." What are your thoughts on this growth in 24/7 tokenization trading?

【Santi】: I mentioned this when we chatted in Brooklyn before; I'm essentially a "long volatility" person. I still hold that view. The trading desire is ingrained in the genes of the younger generation; they want to trade anytime, anywhere.

This desire manifests in several forms. First, in predicting markets, such as Polymarket. Second, in more macro-level asset trading.

The reality is, not everyone is interested in your new generation of currency, but they want to trade commodities. When macro enthusiasts like Stan Druckenmiller say to go long on copper, or geopolitics causes oil prices to surge, traders need an outlet. SuperLiquid made that money. While I haven't seen the final audited figures, I'd wager that its commodity open interest (OI) could grow 10-fold or even 100-fold within a year after breaking a certain threshold. Last weekend, when tensions were high in the Middle East, I sat with a group of hedge fund managers, these brightest microprocessors worrying about what would happen when the market opened on Monday. I simply pulled out my phone and showed them SuperLiquid's live charts; oil and related indices were already fluctuating. Their eyes practically popped out of their sockets, exclaiming, "What the hell is this?" It feels like I've only been there six years ago when stablecoins were first invented. There was no DeFi term yet, and I told potential investors: just try investing in a stablecoin like this, and you'll see the dawn of finance. Now, Hyperliquid is giving people a glimpse of the future of trading. [Host]: We've seen the SEC and CFTC recently begin collaborating on guidance regarding tokenized securities and commodities. Do you think the future of token economics will modify stock structures? For example, will we see "growth tokens" and "dividend tokens"? If regulators suggest paying dividends to token holders through on-chain revenue, is that the right way to capture value? [Santi]: I'm not a regulatory expert and can't predict every detail of legal compliance. But I think Hyperliquid's narrative is powerful because it's very pure and easy to understand. The human brain can only grasp one core concept at a time. If a token has overly complex logic, people tend to avoid it. What has Hyperliquid done? They have real trading volume, they use revenue for atonement, their compensation fees—it's currently the most "stock-like" token. Behind it lie those mystical narratives, directly implying the controversy surrounding global commodities. Moreover, you should note that commodity volatility is more persistent than that of individual stocks and is influenced by geopolitics. When this demand is combined with a 24/7 on-chain infrastructure foundation, its explosive power is astonishing. III. Investment Gap: When Geniuses Are All Going to AI [Host]: You said you almost never invest in angel rounds anymore, but your previous company, ParaFi, just raised $450 million. If you were given several hundred million dollars in the current environment, where would you invest it? Market prediction, AI, or pure crypto infrastructure? [Santi]: That is indeed a difficult question. Sometimes I want to just say, "There's nothing to invest in right now." While projects like Polymarket, Kalshi, or the crypto credit card project Rain are still raising large sums, these are already successful, upgraded projects. In my most active early stages—the stage of making my first and second checks—high-quality projects definitely decreased. I reflected on whether I was too pessimistic, causing entrepreneurs to stop approaching me? Or whether my reputation had been tarnished? But when I asked other top VCs, the answer was the same: the flow of high-quality deals (projects) is indeed shrinking. I believe my core opportunities will lie at the intersection of FinTech (traditional FinTech) and DeFi. [Host]: Is this boundary disappearing? [Santi]: Yes, they are merging. Look at the institutions that invested in Rain; besides traditional Fintech VCs, there are also crypto-native VCs. In the future, if you don't understand Fintech compliance, issuance, and account systems, you'll find it difficult to survive in the crypto investment field. Another key reason is the siphon effect of AI. The smartest people in our industry are now being attracted by AI. In previous cycles, we didn't have this competitive advantage. I'm fascinated by AI. As an investor, I used to have a severe "imposter syndrome," feeling that since I didn't have a technical background, newspaper companies would definitely need to find a CTO first. But now, with AI tools, if you see a tweet today about hiring, I probably won't write "needs a CTO" or "chief executive officer." AI has drastically reduced the cost of building. However, a side effect is that many crypto projects are now just "patching things up," lacking that flashy innovation. Many teams are simply making adjustments to existing L1/L2. While agentic commerce—or AI agents using stablecoins for automated trading—is an inevitable trend, it's still in its very early stages, and many projects seem destined to be short-lived.

IV. Inversion Project Logic: Acquiring Traditional Mining, Injecting Crypto Soul

【Host】: Let's talk about your new plan—Inversion. You mentioned it's a completely new path, not building a crypto project from scratch, but acquiring traditional companies with distribution channels and traffic, such as telecom companies (Telco) or traditional financial institutions. That sounds very Berkshire Hathaway-esque.

【Santi】: Exactly. One of my core principles is: I'm going to buy things that already have distribution rights. I'm not building distribution rights from scratch because that's too slow and too expensive.

The whole point of Inversion is "Don't do stupid things." Buffett's first deal was a textile mill, a terrible business, but he later bought an insurance company, laying the foundation for his empire. I'm just as obsessive; I don't want to buy a company that will "kill" me. [Host]: You've recently done a lot of research in Latin America, even hiring 20 interns to do street surveys? [Santi]: Yes. If you want to be a better investor, you have to do things that "don't exist online." Everyone's too reliant on AI dashboards, charts, or data analytics tools right now. I know an investor whose charts are gorgeous, but he's never called the founders of any project. There's only one thing AI can't replace: sitting down, looking people in the eye, reading their body language, and feeling their humanity. Our research in Mexico revealed data you'd never get sitting in a Silicon Valley office: how 18- to 25-year-olds use bank accounts, how much data they consume monthly, and their reliance on WhatsApp transfers. However, I found that the market's average valuation for fintech users in Latin America is $1,000 per user. But I could buy a traditional company with 21 million users that hasn't yet "fintech-ized" for less than $300 million. If I could embed stablecoin payments and DeFi, that value difference would represent a huge profit margin. V. The Endgame of On-Chain Lending: Rejecting "Ladder Leverage," Embracing Real Assets [Host]: The "embedded finance" you mentioned, especially the lending component, is probably the most controversial right now. DeFi's TVL is rising, but hacking incidents are also frequent. What are your thoughts on the future of on-chain lending? [Santi]: This is my strongest point of contention. Current DeFi lending is primarily "leveraged lending"—people are borrowing from different mining farms in a loop to earn small rewards. This results in extremely short loan terms and enormous amounts. We lack truly "fixed-rate, fixed-term" on-chain credit. Why? Because no one is bringing high-quality Real-World Assets (RWAs) to the blockchain. Many RWA platforms are currently experiencing severe "adverse selection"—people who can borrow from traditional banks won't come to the blockchain for high-interest loans. The result is that on-chain assets are all high-quality junk that can't be credited in the real world. [Host]: So how does the reverse solve this problem? **Santi:** We want to underwrite it ourselves, placing ourselves in the "First Loss Piece." Take telecom companies as an example; they have receivables from smartphone financing. We can bring these assets across many chains. You're doing credit assessment on a real smartphone and a monthly paying user, which is more skeletal than those ethereal token hosts. We're not in it; we're the owners. This "buy the business first, then encrypt it" approach, I believe, is the true form of institutional adoption. **Host:** Finally, Santi, could you summarize this conference? What are your thoughts on the current state of institutional adoption? Santi: I'm very excited, but it's a calm excitement. I remember the way people looked at me when I left JPMorgan Chase, wearing a suit to buy my first Bitcoin. Now? If you're not wearing a suit, you're the odd one out. Institutional adoption will continue to grow 10x, 100x, but that doesn't mean you can double your money just by buying a token. The real beneficiaries will be entities like Robinhood, Western Union, or the reverse we're building—entities that can significantly improve business efficiency using crypto. For many teams, my advice is: don't rush into issuing a token. Wait six months, wait a year, and observe how regulations evolve. If you don't have real users and scale, issuing a token will hasten your demise. We're openly building all of this, and although the road is difficult, it's really interesting to see this convergence happening. **Host:** A very insightful update from an investor. **Santi:** Thank you for sharing today. See you in the next cycle. **Santi:** Thank you. I hope I'm still alive by then.

data.title: PolyMarket raises $600 million, with 20 projects raising $809.7 million.
data.description: This week, 20 blockchain startups raised a total of $809.7 million. The largest funding round this week saw Polymarket raise $600 million from the Intercontinental Exchange (ICE). Polymarket is a decentralized information marketplace platform where users can trade on highly controversial topics and build portfolios based on their predictions. By allowing users to buy “shares” in the marketplace, Polymarket provides actionable insights based on traders’ views of future events, making it a leading, unbiased, and real-time data source. However, due to regulatory restrictions, the platform is currently unable to operate in the United States, leaving room for other platforms to grow. Verse8 Raises $5 Million in Seed Funding from Neowiz, Story Protocol, Nexpace, Nexus, and MARBLEX Corp. Verse8 is a Web3 platform dedicated to redefining digital interaction through immersive, tokenized experiences. It blends social networking, gamification mechanics, and blockchain-based ownership, enabling users to create, participate in, and monetize content in a decentralized environment. By leveraging on-chain identity and interactive virtual spaces, Verse8 aims to connect entertainment with the digital economy, empowering communities with genuine asset ownership and a participatory ecosystem. Megapot raised $5 million in its pre-seed funding round from Dragonfly Capital, Coinbase Ventures, Bankless Ventures, Josh Hannah, and Nigel Eccles. Megapot is a decentralized lottery platform built on the Base blockchain, offering daily massive jackpots. Liquidity providers can participate as "bookmakers" and earn rewards. The platform aims to become the world's largest lottery system by providing users with a more favorable probability of winning, while allowing developers to build on an immutable, open prize pool. Unlike traditional lotteries that take a cut from the prize pool, Megapot does not charge fees and uses blockchain technology to ensure transparency and fairness. Furthermore, users can benefit from staking rewards from the prize pool funds, thereby increasing the overall value. Kairos Swap raised $2.4 million in its seed round from 6th Man Ventures (6MV), Lattice Fund, Advancit Capital, and Compa Capital. Kairos is an on-chain interest rate swap and prediction market protocol built on Ethereum, enabling users to trade fixed and floating rate exposures across various benchmarks such as lending yields and staking yields. It combines AMM-based liquidity with curated vaults to provide deep aggregated liquidity and efficient execution. With its oracle-agnostic design and ERC-721 tokenized positions, Kairos provides composable fixed-income infrastructure, allowing users to hedge, speculate, and build permissionless interest rate markets in DeFi. Payy raised $6 million in seed funding from FirstMark, DBA, and Robot Ventures. Payy is a privacy-focused stablecoin platform that uses zero-knowledge technology to enable privacy transactions by default. It offers self-custodied wallets and Visa cards, allowing users to seamlessly use USDC while maintaining the privacy of on-chain activities. Payy is also building the Payy Network—an Ethereum Layer 2 Rollup that hides transaction data (including sender, receiver, and amount) using zero-knowledge proofs. With its wallet already online, network about to launch, and token plans, Payy aims to drive mainstream adoption of privacy-focused stablecoin payments. Eunice AI raised $8 million in seed funding from Moonfire Ventures, Speedinvest, Openspace Ventures, and others. Eunice is an AI-driven due diligence and compliance platform serving the digital asset and private equity markets. It focuses on high-risk decision-making processes, providing evidence-based outputs, configurable risk rules, continuous monitoring, and enterprise-grade governance controls. The platform positions itself as a "responsibility layer" between AI systems and human decision-making, emphasizing traceability, verifiable results, privacy protection, and compliance with standards such as SOC 2 Type II and GDPR. Eureka Labs raised $6.7 million in seed funding from Spark Capital, Collider Ventures, and others. Eureka Labs is an Ethereum block builder developing "programmable blocks," which introduce executable logic into the block building process, rather than treating blocks as passive transaction containers. This approach aims to support advanced on-chain use cases such as in-block credit, deterministic transaction ordering, state-based precomputation, and selective access to off-chain data during execution. The project positions the block builder as a more proactive execution layer within the Ethereum proposer-builder ecosystem, aiming to support more complex DeFi strategies, trading applications, and next-generation smart contract workflows. Tori Finance raised an undisclosed amount in its seed round from Delphi Ventures. Tori Finance is a DeFi protocol focused on optimizing yield strategies through automated vaults and structured financial products. Users can deposit assets and use curated strategies to maximize returns while controlling risk. Through smart contract automation, Tori simplifies complex DeFi operations, making yield acquisition more accessible to both casual and advanced users. The platform emphasizes efficiency, transparency, and composability. PeakAI raised $2 million in seed funding from multiple institutions. PeakAI is an AI-driven platform that combines Web3 analytics and decentralized marketing infrastructure. It transforms blockchain data into actionable insights and enables brands to conduct distributed marketing campaigns with micro-influencers paid in USDC. Through proprietary AI models and the Kindle scoring system, the platform evaluates creator performance based on real engagement and reach. It automates campaign execution, reward distribution, and performance tracking, enabling brands to gain quantifiable influence while allowing creators to monetize efficiently. Other Funding Rounds: Tazapay raised $36 million in Series B funding from Circle Ventures and others. Tazapay is a global cross-border payment infrastructure platform that provides seamless local collection and payment services in over 70 markets. It supports businesses, B2B marketplaces, and fintech companies, covering debit cards, alternative payment methods, virtual accounts, bank transfers, and stablecoins. By bridging fiat and stablecoins, it enables compliant digital settlements in emerging markets. It is currently licensed in Singapore, Canada, and the EU, and is applying for licenses in the US, UAE, Hong Kong, and Australia. XFX raised $17 million in its Series A funding round from Castle Island Ventures and others. XFX is an institutional-grade cross-border settlement platform that supports real-time, transparent flows of fiat and stablecoins. It provides businesses with deep liquidity, global instant settlement, and enterprise-grade audit and reporting tools, and complies with SOC2 and ISO 27001 standards. ForgeX raised funds from Sonic SVM in an acquisition funding round (amount undisclosed). ForgeX is a Web3 SDK that simplifies the building, deployment, and management of blockchain applications, providing APIs, automation tools, and infrastructure support. AtlasOra raised $5 million in a public sale funding round. AtlasOra is a stablecoin settlement protocol built on Base that converts idle prepaid escrow funds into on-chain yields through Aave. Startale Labs raised $50 million from SBI Holdings in a Series A funding round. Startale Labs is a Web3 technology company focused on multi-chain application development, infrastructure construction, and project incubation. Its products include the popular Japanese Astar Network. Watch.fun raised $8.6 million from investors including Crypto.com Capital. Watch.fun is an on-chain platform that combines luxury watch collecting with real-time social entertainment. Ledger raised $50 million. Ledger is a Paris-based company whose hardware wallets are used for offline storage of private keys to crypto assets. Rakkar Digital received funding in an acquisition financing round (undisclosed). Rakkar Digital is a crypto asset custody platform primarily serving institutions in Southeast Asia. IDEX raised funds in an acquisition funding round (undisclosed). IDEX is a decentralized exchange combining a centralized order book and an Automated Market Maker (AMM). Catapult raised funds in a funding round (undisclosed). Catapult is a DeFi platform built on HyperEVM, combining liquidity launches and trading infrastructure. Origins Network raised $8 million. Origins Network is a blockchain infrastructure project that provides a continuously running, modular execution environment for AI agents and autonomous systems, with a focus on verifiable computation and security.

data.title: Harness has become a sensation—but people don't understand who it's really trying to devour.
data.description:
In Palo Alto this morning, as his coffee was being served, Alan Walker glanced at Anthropic's article on harnesses, looked up, and said only one sentence:

Many people think this is another improvement in the model. Wrong. This is...the process is starting to betray people."

On the surface, this article talks about engineering design, planners, generators, evaluators, and how to get Claude to run continuously for hours to create more complex products.

Most people stop reading here.

They might think: "Oh, so the agent is just more complex, the prompts are longer, and the workflow is more detailed." But Alan says, what's truly worth looking at is never the surface-level functionality, but rather where power is shifting. In the past, to complete a complex task, someone had to break down the requirements, someone had to execute, someone had to check, someone had to rework, and someone had to cover for failures. Now, Anthropic isn't making the model more like a smart employee; it's letting the entire system take over the organizational, supervisory, and acceptance rights that originally belonged to humans. Harness isn't an add-on. Harness is the machine starting to grow a "management layer." That's what's truly frightening.

01 Not a tool, but "the layer that manages tools"

Many people, upon seeing "harness," immediately think: Isn't this just another agent framework?

This understanding is too superficial.

The essence of a normal tool is to listen to commands and then execute them. You click, it does something. You don't say anything, it doesn't do anything.

But harnesses are no longer based on this logic.

What it truly does is to software-ify the division of labor that was originally hidden within human teams: Who understands the requirements, who breaks them down into stages, who executes, who checks, and who has the authority to rework after discovering problems. In other words, Anthropic isn't just adding more features; it's writing the very concept of "how to organize work" into the system. Why is this step important? Because what's hardest to replicate in the past has never been a single skill, but rather organizational ability. Many people can write code. But very few can organize a dozen people, a dozen steps, a dozen rounds of rework, and ultimately deliver a stable delivery. And harnesses deal precisely with this most expensive element. Tools improve efficiency, organization determines output. A single model is merely labor; Harness began to grasp the company structure. When AI not only does the work, but also begins to delegate tasks, hand over responsibilities, and assign accountability, it becomes far more than just a simple "tool upgrade."

02 It's not about being smarter, it's about being less likely to fail.

The most deceptive thing about models is that they always seem very smart in short tasks.

Ask them a question, and they answer it perfectly; ask them to write a piece of code, and they often do a decent job. So many people mistakenly think: since they can do short tasks, long tasks are just about running for a little longer, right?

Not at all.

The real difficulty in long tasks is never about not being able to do a certain step, but about maintaining consistency, control, and self-deception after dozens of consecutive steps.

The same applies to humans doing projects.

The worst thing isn't not knowing how, but getting confused as you go on: You can't remember the requirements, the goals start to drift, the logic becomes inconsistent, and ultimately, what you're best at isn't finishing the task, but writing a summary that looks like it's done. The core problem mentioned in Anthropic's article is essentially this: Models gradually lose their spirit in long-term tasks. The longer the context, the more chaotic the state, and the easier it is to prematurely fall into a "good enough" mental illusion. Harness's value isn't in making it more agile, but in making it less scattered, less vague, and less easily fooled. Decomposing phases, handover, contract signing, independent evaluation, and failure rollback—these may seem like process details, but they all address the same underlying problem: Intelligence can be unstable, but delivery cannot rely on luck. Therefore, to truly understand harnesses, you must first understand one thing: What will truly be valuable in the future is not someone who occasionally produces a stunning demo. It's someone who can consistently push things forward for hours, days, or even longer without leaving a mess. Being able to write code isn't rare. What's rare is writing code that doesn't crash at the end. A flash of inspiration is worthless; stable delivery is valuable. Alan said that the most chilling blow in the Anthropic paper wasn't to the planner or the generator, but to the evaluator. Why? Because large models have a problem remarkably similar to humans: they always feel their work is acceptable. Without external constraints, it's easy for it to give a self-evaluation of "overall good," "basically complete," or "core functionality is already in place." The problem is that this kind of evaluation is often not a lie, but a kind of systemic self-forgiveness. In human companies, why do so many projects ultimately fail? Because the people doing the work are often the best at finding excuses for themselves. The person who made it said it was almost done, the person who inspected it was too lazy to look at it closely, and so a "almost done" thing was approved all the way through, only to explode when it reached the user. Anthropic's ruthless approach is to break the process down into two parts: One role does the work, the other identifies the mistakes. The former is responsible for driving the process forward, the latter for questioning. The logic behind this is very deep: Once the right to produce and the right to evaluate are separated, the system truly begins to form a closed loop. And what's even more frightening is that Anthropic doesn't just let evaluators say a few words like "I think this is bad." It tries to structure the "error-finding" process as much as possible: Functionality needs to be tested, page details need to be checked, APIs need to be examined, database status needs to be reviewed, and design quality is also broken down into scoreable dimensions. What does this mean? This means that many judgments that were once mystified by humanity are being gradually broken down into processes, standards, and thresholds. What's often automated first isn't physical labor, but rather fault-finding. Once the question of "whether this thing is acceptable or not" becomes a process, many people's experience-based advantages will begin to leak. In the past, many jobs were truly valuable not because of production skills, but because of the right to say "whether this thing is acceptable or not." Now, this power is beginning to loosen its grip on people. The most ruthless blow is forbidding it from praising itself. Alan says that the coldest blow in the Anthropic paper isn't the planner or the generator, but the evaluator. Why? Because large models have a problem very similar to humans: they always think what they've done is okay. Without external constraints, it's easy for it to give a self-evaluation of "overall good," "basically completed," and "core functions are already in place." The problem is that this evaluation is often not a lie, but a kind of systemic self-forgiveness. Why do so many projects in human companies ultimately fail? Because those who actually do the work are often the best at making excuses for themselves. The person who does the work says it's almost done, the person who reviews it is too lazy to look closely, and so a "almost done" thing gets approved all the way through, only to explode when it reaches the user. One of Anthropic's ruthless aspects is that it directly breaks this process down: One role is responsible for doing the work, and another role is responsible for finding fault. The former is responsible for pushing things forward, and the latter for questioning. The logic behind this is very deep: Once the production rights and evaluation rights are separated, the system truly begins to form a closed loop. And what's even more frightening is that Anthropic doesn't just let evaluators say a few words like "I think this is bad." It tries to structure the "fault-finding" process as much as possible: Functionality is tested, page details are checked, APIs are examined, database status is reviewed, and design quality is broken down into scoreable dimensions. What does this mean? This means that many judgments that were once mystified by humanity are being gradually broken down into processes, standards, and thresholds. The first thing to be automated is often not physical labor, but fault-finding. Once the question of "whether this thing is good or not" becomes a process, many people's experience-based advantages will begin to leak. In the past, many jobs were truly valuable not because of their production capabilities, but because of their right to say "whether this thing is good or not." Now, this power is beginning to loosen from people's hands. 04. The first to be devoured are not programmers, but those who think "good enough is good enough." Upon seeing articles like this, many people's immediate reaction is: Are programmers doomed? Alan says that this kind of question is too superficial and too lazy. Harness's first wave of attacks didn't target a specific job title. It first devours a long-standing and common survival strategy in almost all knowledge work:

If the requirements are unclear, start doing it; If things go wrong along the way, fix it later; The effect is average, but it runs; The documentation isn't clear, but everyone on the team understands; "Go live first, fix problems later." To put it bluntly, this is a whole set of working methods based on fuzzy space and human flexibility. Many projects can still move forward not because the process is truly clear, but because someone always manages to fill in the gaps through experience, filling in gaps, and making ad-hoc judgments. Harness is doing exactly the opposite. It is compressing the fuzzy space. It is compressing the interface space. First, define what constitutes "done" in this round, then allow work to begin; if the work doesn't meet the requirements, reject it; if the verification fails, continue; don't rely on feelings, rely on evidence. Once this logic is implemented, the most dangerous people are never the best coders, but rather those who rely most on the gray areas for survival. Harness didn't eat up programmers; he ate up ambiguity first. Not everyone will be replaced, but every position that survives on ambiguity will depreciate first. Many jobs used to survive on information asymmetry; many jobs will die from standard deviation in the future. 05 Why is it suddenly popular now? Many people will ask, "These workflow-based things have been done before, why is everyone taking them seriously this time?" Because the underlying model wasn't strong enough before. To put it more bluntly: Many frameworks like this used to look beautiful, but were cumbersome to run, and ultimately not robust enough. You build a bunch of processes, add a bunch of roles, write a bunch of rules, and in the end, you're just packaging an unreliable model into a more complex and unreliable system. So it's understandable that many people lost patience with the agent, workflow, and scaffold concept in the past. It wasn't that the direction was wrong, but rather that the foundation hadn't reached that stage. Now it's different. Once the model crosses a certain threshold, many processes that were previously just decorative begin to unleash their true value for the first time. Because when the underlying model is strong enough, the process is no longer supporting a useless system, but rather amplifying a system that was already capable of continuous operation. This is why harnesses suddenly seem "more real" now. It's not that its concept just appeared today, but rather that the model has finally become strong enough to reap the benefits of the process. Alan's statement is quite accurate: "Model capability is the engine, Harness is the transmission. Before a good engine, even the best transmission is useless. But when the engine is powerful enough, the transmission begins to determine who can reach the highway and who is still revving the engine in place." So this wave isn't just a simple technological trend; it's the industry sending a deeper signal: Future competition won't just be about who has the stronger model, but about who can integrate that model into their production system first.

06 "People are assumed to stand in the middle"

Finally, Alan put down the cup and said the coldest sentence of the day:

"Before, people stared at the software to do the work; from now on, the software will stare at the software to do the work."
